original_abstract: This article explores the transformative impact of the Circle of Concerned African Women Theologians in gender-mainstreaming higher theological education across the continent. Grounded in the framework of African Women’s Theology, the study analyzes the organization’s initiatives in championing gender equality, inclusivity, and intersectional solidarity within the academic sphere. It highlights the Circle’s historical journey in promoting gender-sensitive curricula and mentoring a new generation of African women scholars. Employing a qualitative research design, the study utilizes African Women’s Theology as an interpretive lens to evaluate the significant strides made by the Circle toward dismantling patriarchal structures and fostering a more equitable theological landscape in Africa.
humanized_abstract: Gender transformation in the Circle of Concerned African Women Theologians through Mainstreaming Gender in Higher Theological Education in Africa The study aims to highlight the gender transformation through mainstreaming gender in higher theological education achieved by the Circle of Concerned African Women Theologians. Taking the framework of African Women’s Theology into account, the study examined efforts the Circle has undertaken toward realisation of gender equity, equality, and solidarity within the context of education generally and higher theological education in particular, while mentoring young African women for an equal role as scholars within academia. This study explored the impact of the Circle of Concerned African Women Theologians on mainstreaming gender in higher theological education through the African Women’s Theology prism and its struggle in eliminating men from the theological institution at Africa in a qualitative study research approach.
